entry.title: Filters Not Applied as Expected When Using Model Functions in PaPM Cloud Universal Model
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

In PaPM on-prem, the modeler knows that the package selections used in process templates are applied as filters during function runtime execution. Filter values maintained in the package selection fields are dynamically applied as filters in the modeling setup.

For instance, if a processing function is executed using a process selection field with Quarter = ‘Q3’, and the field Quarter is present in the modeling setup, the value provided is automatically applied as a filter across the overall modeling setup during execution without requiring any additional complex selection filter configuration. This reduces the processing of irrelevant records during function execution and calculation. As a result, modeling performance improves significantly, leading to faster execution times.

Coming to PaPM Cloud Universal Model, UM supports modeling setups using both normal functions (that is, non-model functions such as Calculation, Join, View, etc.) and Model Functions.

In PaPM Cloud Universal Model, I observed unexpected behavior when using Model Functions as part of the modeling setup. When Model Functions are used as the core of the modeling setup, the package selection filters used in the process do not work as expected.

The expected behavior is that filters should be applied prior to the calculation during function execution. However, the actual behavior is that the filters are applied only after the calculation, not before.

Let me explain this behavior using a simple business example for better understanding.

  • Modeling Setup 1 – Expected Behavior
  • Modeling Setup 2 – Actual Behavior

The business example is to calculate the Total Discount Amount for High-Value Orders. Two tables are used: CustomersModelEntity and OrdersModelEntity.

 

 

 

The goal is to calculate the total discount amount for a selected customerGroup and a defined orderAmount range. In this example, a static 10% discount is used in the formula to calculate the discountedOrderAmount.

Modeling Setup 1 – Expected Behavior (without using Model Functions)

To calculate the discountedOrderAmount for the selected customerGroup and defined orderAmount range during processing, a calculation function is used.

This calculation function calculates the totalDiscount amount for the selected customerGroup and defined orderAmount range during processing.

The function is executed using activities created for the writer function along with package selections where:

  • customerGroup = ‘Retail’
  • orderAmount between 10,000 and 50,000

Below is the expected result screenshot after successful execution using the package selection fields.

Modeling Setup 2 – Actual Behavior (when using Model Functions)

The below screenshot shows the Model Calculation function, which is used to calculate the discountedOrderAmount using the same formula as in the expected behavior modeling setup for the selected customerGroup and defined orderAmount range.

The Model Calculation function is then used to calculate the totalDiscount amount using the same formula as in the expected behavior modeling setup.

The function is executed using activities created for the writer function along with package selections where:

  • customerGroup = ‘Retail’
  • orderAmount between 10,000 and 50,000

Below is the actual result screenshot after successful execution using the package selection fields.

Here, the expected result is 4,000, which is the correct aggregation of the discounted amount for the selected customerGroup and defined orderAmount range. However, in Modeling Setup 2, what actually happens is that the calculation defined in the Model Functions is executed first, and only in the final result are the filter values from the activity selection applied.

As a result, the value 6,800 is shown, which represents the aggregation of all discounted amounts irrespective of customer group and other filters.

I am wondering whether this is the intended behavior of Model Functions, or if something needs to be enhanced or fixed in the Universal Model component. If any SAP UM experts read this blog, please share your thoughts.

Conclusion

When using Model Functions in the modeling setup, filters are not properly applied during function execution. The filters are applied only after the calculation, which leads to incorrect results. To avoid such issues, the recommended approach is to use normal functions instead of Model Functions for processing.

If anyone has faced a similar challenge, please share the workaround you have used. Also, feel free to leave a comment if you have any questions or suggestions.

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: What’s New as of 2026-03-02
entry.content:
Joule | AI Enhancements


Several improvements have been introduced to make working with Joule more flexible, intuitive, and user-friendly, with a strong focus on giving users more control.

What’s improved:


• Use Joule Without Saving a Report: You can now start using Joule immediately without first saving a report, allowing you to gain insights faster directly from your current context.

• Ask Joule About Charts: Joule can now understand the data behind charts, allowing you to ask questions about trends, comparisons, or calculated values shown in a visualization.

• Stop an Ongoing Joule Response: Users can now interrupt Joule while it is generating and streaming a response, providing more control over the interaction.


• Reasoning AI Model: From the Settings screen, you can now select a specific Reasoning AI Model for complex questions, while the Default AI Model handles faster tasks like chat title generation.


For an overview of supported and recommended AI models, see Supported Foundation Models.

 

Process Management | Download and Upload of Process Instances


We have enhanced the XLSX download and upload experience to improve operational efficiency for scenarios like daily closing chains.


• Consolidated XLSX Download: Downloading multiple selected process instances now produces one consolidated XLSX file instead of a ZIP containing multiple files.

• Source Process Mapping: The downloaded file includes a new “Source Process” column that stores the originating process ID for each instance.


• Flexible Uploads: The Upload action is now available even when no process is selected. If the XLSX contains the “Source Process” column, the system creates instances based on that mapping.


Why it matters


This improves operational efficiency for scenarios like daily closing chains by reducing repetitive uploads, improving consistency across templates, and lowering the chance of format-related errors.

For more information about this update, see Process Management.

 

Modeling Screen | Processing Functions


With the latest update, the new modeling function design is now the standard UI. Previously offered as an optional feature, this design moves the Input, Lookup, Signature, Output, and Checks node to the properties panel allowing you to view and update all function settings in one place.

 

 

For more information, see Function Building Blocks.

Message Logs


We also have enhanced the message logs from modeling and other areas of the application. Now, when the message log shows more than 50 lines from singular, multiple, or whole environment activation or run, then a redirection to Application Monitor will be shown.


We aim to make sure the specific Run ID is focused to ensure that users will be able to see all relevant messages from a particular action and avoid missing an important line.

 

RFA | Improved Logging Visibility


We’ve improved RFA logging visibility by enriching the results (Y-table) with additional posting messages on top of the existing Application Monitor updates.

Header-level Outcomes: Technical messages like “Document posted successfully” are now written into the technical field FS_PER_MSG_TEXT_.

 

 

Easier Tracing: This enhancement makes it easier to trace posting feedback directly on the Data Management screen (with Show technical fields enabled) without relying solely on application logs.


Built-In Help and Notifications

 

SAP Companion in Visualize: The Built-In Help, also known as SAP Companion, is now accessible within the Visualize screen to provide guided information and detailed descriptions to enhance your user experience.


Expanded Notifications: You can now receive both in-app and email notifications when a scheduled job run starts and completes, provided these channels are activated in your user settings.

For detailed guidance on how to subscribe to notifications, please refer to the Notifications documentation.

That’s a wrap! 🎁

Thank you for reading. We hope these updates help make your day-to-day work in SAP PaPM Cloud even smoother. Stay tuned for the next edition of our “What’s New” series!

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model What’s New as of 2026-03-02
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

As we step into a new month, it’s time to highlight the latest updates and improvements introduced to the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM). In this edition of What’s New, I’ll guide you through the newest enhancements designed to support more efficient modeling, better transparency, and an overall smoother experience.

If you’d like to explore previous updates from the team, feel free to check out all past posts using the PaPMCloudUMWhatsNew tag.

Model

Enhanced Read Access for Protected Environments

When working in protected environments, users often notice that certain parts of the system are restricted by design. This is intentional - these environments are designed to safeguard sensitive customer data. But it also means users have limited visibility into what’s happening behind the scenes. 

When protection is turned on, several important technical details are hidden, such as: 

  • The full list of functions (active or inactive) in Manage Functions and Show Model 
  • All connections 
  • All fields 
  • Data of any internal functions 
  • The ability to run internal functions

While this protects sensitive information, it can add complexity to troubleshooting for both customers and SAP support - often requiring customer data to be exported and restored into a DEV tenant with an unprotected environment.

To improve the experience, the system was adjusted so that users with MODEL_ALL / MODEL roles  –  even with protection set to Yes – would get read access to:

  • Manage Environments

  • Manage Functions

  • Manage Fields

  • Manage Connections

Note: Sensitive items like model filters and the list of models will stay hidden. 

  • Manage Partitions

This helps users see the list of functions and the basic setup, making issue analysis much easier.

To keep the environment secure, areas that contain detailed function configuration logic remain protected and not accessible. Specifically, this refers to:  

  • Manage Model Entities 
  • Manage Model HANAs 
  • Manage Model References 
  • Manage Views and Manage Model Views 
  • Manage Joins and Manage Model Joins 
  • Manage Connections and Manage Model Connections 
  • Manage Derivations and Manage Model Derivations 
  • Manage Calculations and Manage Model Calculations 
  • Manage Allocations and Manage Model Allocations 
  • Manage Currency Conversions and Manage Model Currency Conversions 
  • Manage Unit Conversions and Manage Model Unit Conversions 
  • Manage Transfer Structures and Manage Model Transfer Structures 
  • Manage Collections and Manage Model Collections 
  • Manage Readers 
  • Manage Writers

And other areas that expose detailed function information.

Even in protected mode, users can still run functions with API-State = ACTIVE through Manage Activities, just as they do today. 

In essence, the updated behavior is designed to create a more effective balance by providing:

  • Greater transparency for troubleshooting
  • Reduced reliance on exporting data to a development tenant 
  • Continued protection of sensitive internal model information

This makes protected environments safer and more usable.

Value Help for Client Field in Conversion Functions

A new enhancement has been introduced to improve how the Client field is maintained in Conversion functions. With this update, the Client field now provides a Value Help option that allows users to select a value instead of typing it manually. 

 The Client value can now be assigned from: 

  • A field available in the input function
  • A parameter defined in the related environment 

This improvement ensures consistent client assignment and helps the system select the correct records from conversion tables based on tenant-specific data.

The enhancement is applied to the following functions: 

  • Currency Conversion 
  • Model Currency Conversion 
  • Unit Conversion 
  • Model Unit Conversion 

This change improves accuracy, reduces manual effort, and ensures consistent behavior across all conversion processes. 

That wraps up this edition!

We hope you enjoy exploring the latest features and improvements delivered in SAP PaPM Cloud UM. Don’t forget to check back next month for more updates.

Thank you for taking the time to read, and feel free to share this post with your network.

entry.title: Customizing Remote Function Adapter's Component List for SAP PAPM On Premise
entry.content:
Profitability and Performance Management (PAPM) offers a list of Remote Function Adapter (RFA) types which can be used to communicate to other applications and systems. This is done by reading or by posting data, on the same system as PAPM, or on remote systems (for example, SAP ERP or SAP S/4HANA). 

Each RFA type comes with a predefined list of fields, show as components on the function's Rules Tab. To these components, the user can map fields which provide input values or store output results. The RFA works as a wrapper, by calling SAP BAPIs, custom function modules or OData services, and mapping its list of components to the corresponding importing and exporting parameters. 

Until now, to extend a function's list of components in order to meet the specific needs of a user, the user would create a new feature request here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/3041.

To address the users' demands for flexibility and efficiency, I will guide you on how to customise the list of RFA components, and add new fields to it. 

1. Identifying the SAP function module called by the RFA function. 

Use the table below to find which BAPI is called by your RFA type. 

RFA typeTechnical abbreviationSAP Function ModuleNote
Finance Accounts PayableFI_APBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance Accounts Payable ItemsFI_APIBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance Accounts ReceivableFI_ARBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance Accounts Receivable ItemsFI_ARIBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance ExtendedFI_EXTBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance General LedgerFI_GLBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Finance General Ledger ItemsFI_GLIBAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST 
Purchase OrderPOBAPI_PO_CREATE1Limitation: systems with version of S/4 HANA OP1610 or lower - see details below
Primary Cost RepostingPRBAPI_ACC_PRIMARY_COSTS_POSTLimitation: systems with version of S/4 HANA OP1610 or lower - see details below

Sales and Distribution

type = 'SALESORDER'

type = 'QUOTATION'

type = 'INQUIRY'

SD

 

BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2

BAPI_QUOTATION_CREATEFROMDATA2

BAPI_INQUIRY_CREATEFROMDATA2

Limitation: systems with version of S/4 HANA OP1610 or lower - see details below
Secondary Cost Element PostingSPBAPI_ACC_MANUAL_ALLOC_POST 

 

2. Identifying the function module parameter and field you need

Let's have a simple example, where the user wants to add the field 'Company ID of Trading Partner' to RFA FI_AP. 

  • On your target system, run transaction SE37.
  • Fill in function module name - BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST and click the Display button (Figure 1). 
  • Go to tab called Tables (Figure 2).
  • Double click on the associated type BAPIACGL09, corresponding to parameter name ACCOUNTGL.
  • Identify the field TRADE_ID, which has the type CHAR 6, decimals 0, and the description 'Company ID of Trading Partner' (Figure 3). Copy these field technical details.

 Figure 1

Figure 2

Figure 3

3. Maintaining the RFA customizing view

Before modifying the customizing view, we need to check that the necessary placeholder for our field already exists. This is a mandatory condition for the end user to independently add their desired fields.

A. Identifying the placeholder

  • On your local system, run transaction /N/NXI/P1_MF.
  • Navigate in the tree to 'Remote Function Placeholder'.
  • In the list, search for the Placeholder which has the same exact name as the BAPI parameter you just viewed. For our current example, this is 'ACCOUNTGL' (Figure 4).

 Figure 4

Once we make sure that we have all the necessary information, we can move forward to the final step.

B. Adding the new RFA component.

  • Navigate in the tree to 'Remote Function Adapter Mapping'. 
  • Fill in Remote Function Adapter Type with the value 'FI_AP' (code for Finance Accounts Payable), then press 'Enter'.
  • Press the 'Change' button (or Ctrl+F1) and then the 'New Entries' button (or F5).
  • Add all the necessary technical information for the field. (Figure 5)
    Component: structure_name-field_name. Each field belongs to a group of fields, which are then mapped to a BAPI structure. It helps to follow the examples of the fields already defined.
    Sequence: the order in which the components appear on the application UI, on the RFA Rules tab.
    Placeholder: must have the same technical name as the BAPI's parameter.
    Is Mandatory: when choosing Yes, the component will be colored on the application UI. It will red, when the field is not mapped to a value or an environment field, and green, once the field is mapped.
    Declaration Type: RFA components which will be passed to processing are of type Input. RFA components which represent results of processing are of type Output. 
    DDIC Data Type, Data Length, Data Decimals and Name are all technical information taken from the BAPI field definition. 
  • Press 'Save' and select an appropriate Transport Request.

 Figure 5

After activating the objects and releasing the transport requests, the newly added field can be found on the application UI.

❗Limitations

Please note this user-led customization process has the following limitations:

  1. Only available from some RFAs (listed in the table above), where the functionality is simple enough that it can easily align with the customization. 
  2. Only available for systems with version of S/4 HANA OP1610 or lower, in case of RFA PO, PR, SD. PAPM is optimized for S/4HANA 1610. If S/4HANA Version is higher than 1610, the parameters of SAP function modules may have changed data types. This would require more complex work in order to add new RFA components, not just performing the above steps. 
  3. Only available for fields of BAPI parameters which can already be found in the Placeholders list. Adding a new placeholder would require more complex work, including source code changes.

If you find that you are facing one of the above limitations, please create a new feature request for the enhancement you wish to acquire, here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/3041.

 

By following these steps - identifying the SAP function module name, identifying the parameter name and field technical details, maintaining the RFA's customizing view, you now have everything you need to complete the process from start to finish. Once you implement this guide, you'll save time, gain agency and get better and faster results every time you repeat the process. Start with a field and carefully test the results of your work. 

If you run into questions, drop them in the comments and I am happy to help!

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud UM Modeling Performance Improvement Tips and Tricks
entry.content:
SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM) empowers users to design content‑based applications that efficiently handle high‑volume data calculations and complex processing requirements. By leveraging its flexible and scalable modeling capabilities, SAP PaPM Cloud UM allows organizations to transform large datasets into actionable insights while maintaining performance and reliability.

When it comes to performance, it’s no longer just a nice-to-have but it has become an absolute necessity. In SAP PaPM Cloud UM, achieving faster calculation runs is a key factor in ensuring efficient memory usage, enhancing user experience, and allowing for better data handling as business data continues to grow over time.

So, with all that in mind, this blog post will walk you through some of the performance improvement tips and tricks for SAP PaPM Cloud UM based on existing documentation, smarter data model design, making the most out of parallelization.

To clearly outline the key areas of performance optimization, I’ve organized the discussion into sections. This approach also allows you to easily navigate to the topics most relevant to your specific use case.

  1. General Performance Recommendations
  2. Capacity Sizing
  3. Parallelization
  4. Modeling Optimization
  5. Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

Let us proceed.

I.        General Performance Recommendations

  • HANA SDA is a compelling synchronous remote access feature, but because of that nature, reading or writing fails immediately if the communication with the source system is disrupted for a microsecond, the remote system is down for maintenance, some authorization tokens get expired or access control changes. Therefore, it should be used only in exceptional cases.

    When reading from HANA SDA (Smart Data Access) sources, consider the following: Create statistics for an SDA Virtual table as described in Create Statistics on a Virtual Table or Linked Database | SAP Help Portal.

  • If the Manage Event Logs application has too many entries, you may consider performing event log archiving and deletion. For more information, see Manage Archives.

    You can also maximize archiving via schedule by following the steps described in SAP PaPM Cloud UM’s Event Log Archive and Deletion using SAP Job Scheduling Service.

  • Verify whether the HANA DB parameters have been set correctly as described in SAP Note 2186744. If you have set the STATEMENT_MEMORY_LIMIT parameter in the system, the recommended value is 30% of the global allocation limit (if you have 1 HANA node of 3TB, then it should be at 30% of 3TB, which is approximately 1TB).

  • Use the Manage Event Logs application to analyze the runtime performance KPIs for your process. In the detail screen of the Event Log, you can find information about the time spent on input reading, business processing, and writing to the database for each involved function. You can also find information on the number of processed entries and the allocated memory.

    For more information, see Manage Event Logs.

II.        Capacity Sizing

  • Sizing: Incorrect sizing could also cause memory issues. Perform sizing exercises at different points throughout the project (blueprint, realization, final preparation, go-live, change requests/functional changes post go-live, etc.) and not just once during initial phases. Incorrect sizing would also lead to out-of-memory/performance issues (despite performance tuning of the model) because the available hardware resources would not be enough to run the calculations with a high volume of data. You could consider engaging Expert Sizing Consulting.

  • Before going to production, perform a performance test such as a load/scalability/volume test on a preproduction system. You can use incremental volumes up to production-like volumes to see if the configured model scales linearly, and to get an idea of how much time/memory it will take. This is necessary to ensure that you have performed all necessary performance tuning in your configured calculation model and to confirm whether the sizing of the production system is correct. In such tests, you might realize that the initial/theoretical sizing numbers are not applicable anymore, for example, in case the scenarios/data models/assumptions have changed. As a result, you may need a larger or smaller-sized production system based on the current realizations.

III.        Parallelization

  • The Manage Partitions application allows you to manage large datasets efficiently, optimize performance, and maintain data integrity to maximize your data management capabilities. In the Universal Model application, partitioning is the process of dividing a large dataset into smaller and more manageable pieces of information. This way, the application not only enhances the speed of data processing but also allows for better utilization of system resources.

    For more information, see Manage Partitions and SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model: What’s New as of 2024-10-07?

  • If your calculation model/environment involves writing high-volume data to many model tables and you have implemented packaging using the environment partitioning setting as mentioned in the previous step, consider partitioning these model tables. For SAP PaPM-generated tables (of Model Entity function or any other function's buffer result table), this can be done by maintaining the partitioning ID in the Writer, Partition settings and reactivating the function.

    This will improve the write/insert times considerably and thereby enhance the overall processing time.

  • Apply hash partitioning or, if required, a combined hash-range partitioning on all Writer functions. A suitable number of partitions is 100. In case the Writer writes into a Model Entity, apply the partitioning on the Model Entity. This will reduce the time to write data to database roughly by a factor of 2.

  • When using a Process, always assign a Writer as the "To Activity". With this setup, you can make use of applied hash partitioning to have a significant improvement in terms of performance since you cannot apply Hash Partitioning to other functions, for example Join function.

IV.        Modeling Optimization

  • Avoid unnecessary functions or reduce staging functions in between to reduce materialization of data, which will consume significant memory and lead to longer runtime. In addition, you can combine functions to achieve calculations on the already/same materialized data. Effectively try to reduce the number of functions in the overall environment/calculation model.

  • Maintain filters/selections as early as possible, and apply selections already in the Input tab, if possible, rather than in rules.

  • Reduce the number of columns/fields if they are not really needed in the data you process as early as possible, for example, in cases where most columns are not actually needed during the calculation but were just included for reporting purposes.

  • When modeling a calculation model in SAP PaPM Cloud UM based on previous/legacy solutions, avoid taking over the logic one-to-one. Instead, reduce the number of functions by using SAP PaPM Cloud UM standard functionalities.

  • Use iterative loops with caution and careful consideration (if there are no other SAP PaPM Cloud UM standard options available). Functions or chains of functions that are run within iterative loops should be limited as much as possible.

  • Avoid using nested iterative loops, if possible. Be aware that using nested iterative loops would cost significant time and memory, and the data would grow significantly over every iteration, sometimes exponentially if not carefully configured.

  • To run a function and its related input functions without saving results, set a condition that is never met. This approach saves writing time, especially with high-volume data. The system runs all functions in the input network, but the condition prevents function from selecting any entries, so no data is written to the database.

  • If possible, aggregate data, and reduce granularity as early as possible in the calculation model. This will reduce the number of records to be processed by the calculation model. Verify whether the business scenario/requirement really needs the detailed granularity level of data to be processed throughout the calculation model.

  • For allocation scenarios, check and compare the approach of an account-based allocation vs. a costing/value field-based allocation approach. In certain scenarios, a costing/value field-based allocation approach is more performant/feasible.

  • In an Allocation function, use allocation type “Allocation with detailed offset records” judiciously, and only if needed, as this type produces much more granular results than the other types, resulting in a greater number of records.

V.        Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

1.     Are partitioning settings implicitly applied to all upstream functions leading up to a Writer function that has partitioning configured?

Yes, Hash-Range Partitioning supports the run of the Writer function, including its upstream function chain in sequential levels of parallel range-packages, for example, to save HANA memory or to reduce/control the overall HANA workload, that is, partially sequential or leveled package parallelization.

2.     If partitioning is applied in the Writer function with an Allocation function as input, shall partitioning be applied to the Sender or Receiver input?

If you use Hash-Dynamic or Hash-Range partitioning on the underlying input which is your Model Entity, the reading speed can be improved. If this type of partitioning is applied to the Sender input, then only the Sender reading is improved. If the same partitioning is applied to the Receiver input, then Receiver reading is also improved.

3.    Will an Information function also apply “partitioning”, meaning the SQL statement will be run separately for each partition and then assembled in memory?

Yes, a separate SQL statement is processed per partition. These partitions process different records.

4.    Does partitioning require larger memory utilization for Information functions since it will have a separate SQL statement run for each partition?

Yes, running multiple partitions consumes larger memory utilization in exchange for faster processing time.

That’s all for now! Thank you for taking the time to read the Modeling Performance Improvement Tips and Tricks of SAP PaPM Cloud UM. I hope the insights shared help you enhance and optimize your SAP PaPM Cloud UM.

See you again in the next blog post!

entry.title: How ready are you to administer SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Modeling?
entry.content:
A new learning opportunity has just dropped — and it’s a big one for anyone working with SAP Profitability & Performance Management Cloud!

The brand‑new course Administering SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud: Universal Modeling  is now available in preview on SAP Learning. This hands‑on, role‑based training is designed to help you navigate and administer the Universal Modeling environment with confidence and efficiency.

With this course, you’ll learn how to structure models, manage configurations, streamline data flows, and apply best‑practice governance — all essential skills for professionals looking to elevate their PaPM Cloud expertise. Whether you’re new to Universal Modeling or looking to sharpen your administrative capabilities, this content gives you a solid, practical foundation.

👉Explore the course here

entry.title: SAP PAPM and SAP Business Data Cloud - A Data-Centric Approach to Direct Tax
entry.content:
Picture 1. Jurisdiction tax analysis

MOTIVATION

In today's fast-paced business world, the ability to effectively steer and lead an organization is fundamental for achieving strategic goals and maintaining a competitive edge. A key success criterion is the capacity to fully harness and derive value from organizational data. Many companies struggle with data problems, such as fragmented information, outdated systems, and the super important task of preparing data for emerging technologies like AI. Recognizing these challenges, we are excited to introduce a blog series dedicated to showcasing the substantial business value of SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) in the context of planning and analytics.

The motivation behind this initiative is to provide a comprehensive exploration of how SAP BDC capabilities as essential instruments, facilitating robust strategic alignment and guiding effective decision-making processes across diverse organizational requirements. We aim to illustrate how SAP BDC, equipped with powerful data management, planning, analytics and innovative AI capabilities, empowers organizations with enhanced business steering agility by providing real-time insights, streamlined data management, and automated decision support – going from insights to action, helps mainly leaders guide their businesses with precision.

Our blog series will provide valuable insights for decision-makers seeking to harness SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) to enhance operational efficiency, optimize resource allocation, and achieve long-term objectives. By featuring detailed planning & analytics topics, the series will illustrate the transformative impact of SAP BDC in addressing the operational complexities of today’s business environment. This first introductory blog post will spotlight the strategic advantages of SAP BDC, particularly in the context of Business Steering, setting the stage for a deeper exploration of its benefits throughout the blog series.

There are several blog posts in the series that will cover advantages of SAP BDC in the context of business steering, so please stay tuned for the upcoming blogs:

  1. The Value of SAP BDC in the context of business steering 
  2.  Planning & Analytics is an essential part of SAP BDC  
  3. Key Planning & Analytics components of SAP BDC 
  4. Is AI going to “retire” a dashboard?
  5. Simulations, a game changer in today’s volatile world
  6. AI-Augmented Oil Price Scenario Modelling in SAP Analytics Cloud
  7. Extend existing planning scenarios of SAP BPC/BW-IP in SAP BDC
  8. Real-time steering with LIVE planning
  9. PaPM - The role of business calculation engine in business steering
  10. Self – service analytics and real time business steering
  11. Business steering powered by AI agents
  12. Leverage data products in the context of planning & forecasting
  13. Introducing AI/ML capabilities by SAP Databricks
  14. SAP PAPM and SAP Business Data Cloud - A Data-Centric Approach to Direct Tax (THIS BLOG POST)

INTRODUCTION

Why are we discussing tax in this series, and how does it relate to SAP Business Data Cloud?

Direct tax functions are facing increasing pressure driven by regulatory complexity, shorter reporting cycles, and rising expectations around transparency and auditability. These challenges are not new, but their cumulative impact is becoming harder to manage with fragmented processes and data structures.

Process automation is often seen as a way to address these issues. However, in the context of direct tax, automation is rarely effective on its own. Many limitations stem not from the absence of tools, but from underlying data inconsistencies, limited governance, and restricted reuse of tax-relevant data across systems and processes. Without a solid data foundation, automation efforts tend to remain isolated and require ongoing manual intervention.

From this perspective, SAP Business Data Cloud provides a framework for addressing direct tax challenges by emphasizing data consistency, integration, and reuse. Rather than treating tax as a downstream reporting activity, it enables tax-relevant data to be managed alongside financial and operational data, supporting more reliable calculations, reporting, and compliance processes.

This approach delivers several tangible benefits:

  • Improved data accuracy and consistency across tax, finance, and operational datasets

  • Enhanced auditability and transparency, with clear data lineage and traceability

  • Greater reuse of tax-relevant data, avoiding duplication across systems and processes

  • Stronger alignment between tax and finance, enabling more consistent reporting outcomes

This is why, within SAP, direct tax is increasingly discussed in connection with EPM applications and a shared data foundation. Combining process logic with a consistent data layer helps align financial, tax, and sustainability information from an end-to-end process perspective. It allows organizations to build on ERP data they already rely on, while extending it in a controlled way to support the specific requirements of global tax.

WHERE DATA MEETS A PREDEFINED TAX APPLICATION

When looking at direct tax processes, it quickly becomes clear that many elements are highly standardized. Regulatory frameworks define calculation logic, data requirements, and reporting structures with little room for interpretation. This makes direct tax a strong candidate for predefined, application-based approaches rather than purely bespoke implementations.

This is the approach taken with an SAP PaPM–powered application for direct tax management. The application covers the main steps of the direct tax lifecycle—from data collection and income tax determination to BEPS Pillar Two calculations, reporting, and audit support—while remaining flexible enough to adapt to organization-specific requirements.

Moreover, SAP PaPM enables a creation of a pro-active transfer pricing management framework. While BEPS Pillar Two focuses on minimum taxation and jurisdiction-level Top-Up Tax calculations, Operational Transfer Pricing (OTP) addresses how profits are allocated through intercompany pricing and year-end adjustments. Both areas rely on the underlying financial and transactional data and directly influence each other’s outcomes—as well as the overall bottom line.

This interdependency makes a shared data foundation, provided by SAP Business Data Cloud, particularly important. SAP PaPM runs directly in SAP Datasphere or SAP Hana Cloud and complements this foundation by offering a common calculation and process framework that allows OTP and Pillar Two to be aligned within a single, consistent logic, improving transparency, consistency, and governance across tax processes.

Picture 2. Architecture

To illustrate this, the following sections walk through a BEPS Pillar Two calculation process as it can look like in an SAP-based environment.

Structuring the Pillar Two Process

The process starts from a role-based launchpad, in this example designed for a role of global tax manager. Rather than exposing all technical details, the launchpad focuses on the key steps required in day-to-day user work: data collection, manual adjustments, Safe Harbour tests, elections, and the Top-Up Tax calculation itself.

As a first step master data and annual configuration data play a central role here. These parameters drive the BEPS Pillar Two calculations and can be reviewed and adjusted centrally, ensuring transparency and consistency across jurisdictions and reporting periods.

Picture 3. BEPS Pillar 2 launchpad

Managing Financial Data and Adjustments

Once the configuration is in place, the process moves into financial data processing. BEPS Pillar Two requires significant volumes of data from multiple sources, and many tax-relevant figures must be reviewed or adjusted to align with GloBE definitions.

Manual adjustments are therefore an unavoidable part of the process. The key challenge is not eliminating them, but managing them in a structured and auditable way. In this setup, adjustments are made within a governed environment, with predefined validations, mandatory comments, and a full change history.

For example, tax items can be reviewed by company and scenario. If an adjustment is required—such as modifying related-party revenue for a specific entity—the user can drill down to the relevant tax item, enter the adjustment, and document the rationale. Built-in validations immediately flag inconsistencies, such as incorrect currencies or unusual values, allowing experts to correct or consciously approve deviations.

All changes are traceable: who made them, when, and why. This significantly reduces reliance on external spreadsheets and email-based explanations.

Picture 4. Manual adjustments

Process Transparency and Collaboration

Beyond individual data entries, BEPS Pillar Two is a collaborative, multi-step process. Different roles are involved in data preparation, review, approval, and final calculation.

A process management view provides transparency across these steps. It shows what tasks are pending, who is responsible, and whether approvals are required. This helps avoid the typical coordination challenges that arise when BEPS Pillar Two is managed across disconnected tools and documents.

Picture 5. Process management

From Safe Harbour Checks to Final Results

Once data inputs, adjustments, and elections are completed, the solution supports Safe Harbour assessments. Results can be analyzed at both company and jurisdiction level, showing which jurisdictions pass the Safe Harbour tests and which do not.

For jurisdictions that fail, the system provides an estimated Top-Up Tax result. While this is not the final calculation, it offers early insight into potential exposure and helps tax teams prioritize their efforts.

The full OECD-aligned Top-Up Tax calculation is then performed, considering all configurations, data inputs, and elections. Results are available at jurisdiction and company level, including the allocation of Top-Up Tax under QDMTT and IIR rules and the mechanism through which the tax is payable.

Additional analytical dimensions—such as region or business unit—can be applied to support internal reporting needs.

Picture 6. Top-up Tax allocation results

An important aspect of BEPS Pillar Two compliance is the ability to maintain multiple calculation versions. The solution supports parallel runs, comparisons, and a complete audit trail. This is essential not only for audits, but also for recalculations, restatements, and future regulatory changes.

The focus is not limited to the current reporting cycle, but on creating a sustainable setup that can evolve over time.

In this example, we can see mostly BEPS Pillar Two related data, but as explained in the introduction, SAP PAPM allows you to create a tax management framework, connecting Operational Transfer Pricing and BEPS Pillar Two and supporting forward-looking analysis. As an example, tax teams can simulate how changes in transfer pricing policies or margins affect effective tax rates and potential Top-Up Tax exposure. This moves BEPS Pillar Two from a purely compliance-driven exercise toward a more controlled and proactive tax management process and generates even more value for business users.

Insights and AI-Supported Analysis

While detailed results can be reviewed step by step, summarized dashboards provide a consolidated view of key metrics, such as Safe Harbour status or CbCR-related indicators.

At this point, AI-supported interaction becomes relevant. With Joule embedded into the process, users can ask natural-language questions—such as requesting a summary of BEPS Pillar Two results or identifying jurisdictions with the highest Top-Up Tax. Because these insights are derived from governed, tax-specific data, they support data-driven decision-making without replacing expert judgment.

Picture 7. Joule and AI powered analysis

This highlights the role of SAP Business Data Cloud as an enabler for analytics and AI: insights are only as reliable as the underlying data foundation.

There are also many developments to look forward to on the AI roadmap. Planned features include capabilities such as LLM functions with grounding, enabling the derivation of applicable tax rules based on accounting document descriptions and providing relevant recommendations. Additional innovations include automated process and model generation, as well as a knowledge graph function that will, for example, help validate derived tax rules against the organization’s compliance framework. These advancements are expected to significantly expand the possibilities for AI-driven analysis and automation.

CONCLUSION: PROCESS AND DATA AS EQUAL ENABLERS

The example shown demonstrates an end-to-end BEPS Pillar Two solution that supports tax data management, Safe Harbour assessments, elections, and jurisdiction-level Top-Up Tax calculations within a single, structured framework.

Challenges such as fragmented data, manual reconciliations, and limited transparency are addressed through a combination of predefined process logic and a shared data foundation. Rather than focusing on automation alone, the approach emphasizes consistency, traceability, and reuse of trusted ERP data. It is also important to highlight that SAP PaPM is an agnostic solution. Data can be collected and integrated from multiple sources, including both SAP and non-SAP systems.

This is where SAP PaPM, application-based tax content, and SAP Business Data Cloud complement each other: process logic and data foundation work together to support compliance, analysis, and future adaptability in an increasingly complex tax and EPM landscape.

USEFUL LINKS

In case you are interested in knowing more please refer to our help content for SAP PAPM.

Moreover I would recommend checking other SAP powered tax and analytical solutions to get a full perspective about SAP capabilities in these areas.

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud – Standard Model: Mapping of Source Fields into Environment Fields
entry.content:
With continuous efforts to enhance the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM) modeling experience, one of the most impactful enhancements that is newly introduced is the Mapping of Source Fields into Environment Fields capability. This feature enables a controlled and consistent way of mapping source fields with the environment fields which is used across functions and rules. For QA engineers, architects, and modelers alike, this means fewer discrepancies during execution, and cleaner model governance. In this article, I’ll walk through what this feature enhancement does, why it matters, and how it improves the overall modeling workflow in PaPM Cloud Standard Model.

What this feature enhancement does?

One key benefit of this enhancement is to provide an early warning to modelers in case of manually adding fields based on the source fields. If Source Fields data length is 50 then data length of the Environment Fields should be 50 or higher. Else, a validation will be seen, and modelers will not be able to finalize the mapping unless validation is fixed.

The rule, if Source Fields is with data length 23 then data length of the Environment Fields should be 23 or higher stays true. Therefore, in the fields dropdown what modelers will only see are the compatible environments fields.

This is also true for Key Figure fields for example DEC data type.

If Source Fields in this case the data length is 23 then data length of the Environment Fields should be 23 or higher. Else, a validation will be seen, and modelers will not be able to finalize the mapping unless validation is fixed.

In the event that modelers will be using fields dropdown option, meaning if there are existing environment fields in the modeling screen and modelers would like to reuse them to map the source fields, take note of the sources and environment fields Data Type, Data Length, and Data Decimals compatibility. In this example, for an existing DEC fields:

1 with lesser data length and decimals and 1 with higher data length and decimals.

The rule, if Source Fields is with data length 23 then data length of the Environment Fields should be 23 or higher stays true. Therefore, in the fields dropdown what modelers will only see are the compatible environments fields.

To know more about SAP PaPM Cloud SMs’ Data Types, maximum Data Length, and Data Decimals you may visit the Application Help Data Types, Master and Hierarchy Data.

Why it matters?

With the mapping enhancement in place, the goal is to promote consistent and compatible mapping between source and environment fields to avoid discrepancies during execution and to avoid mapping mistake such as choosing incompatible Data Type and mistakenly overwriting the existing Data Length or Data Decimals of a field which may results to inconsistent calculation.

How it improves the overall modeling workflow?

The keyword is consistency, when modelers have consistent mapping compatibility between source and environment fields, modelers will have the confidence that the data projected from any modeling functions are correct, formulas and selections are aligned, and execution of calculation to run smoothly eliminating the risk of having incorrect Data Type, Data Length, and Data Decimals.

I hope this article provides you with things you need to know about the enhanced Mapping of Source Fields into Environment Fields capability of SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Standard Model.

See you in the next!

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud UM: Utilizing of Parameter model in Writer function
entry.content:
Friendly Reminder

A basic understanding of APIs may be helpful to fully appreciate the context of this technical blog post.

Hi Everyone

SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM) offers powerful writing capabilities that let you transmit payloads to external applications via Connection, whether to SAP Analytics Cloud models, SOAP services, or OData services.

Now, I am here to share a short blog post highlighting how you can seamlessly configure a Parameterized model using Connections on write enabled APIs.

Before we proceed, let me first provide some context on parameterized models that leverage write enabled APIs.

When an OData Service Connection is created in SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model, it defines all available set of fields and parameters that can be used during modeling covering both fields for reading and the required parameters for writing.

These available sets of fields and parameters are being converted as a Model Entity from a successful Connection creation. To give you an example, let’s use this SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SAP SCT) API which is /GetQuantitativeMetricData.

For more information about this API, please see SAP SCT Analytical Use Case.

Using this GetQuantitativeMetricData POST request in SAP PaPM Cloud UM Connection will look like the image below. To have a better understanding, I have also added a brief description for each sections that are highlighted in the image.

  1. This was identified based from the SAP SCT Analytical Use Case which the POST request endpoint is GetQuantitativeMetricData .
  2. When you create the OData Service connection, SAP PaPM Cloud UM reads the $metadata and understands the fixed structure if the POST request requires Parameter Model.

    Therefore, the OData Service connection created will be defining the Parameter Model in its column.

  3. Once you have the details from the Parameter Model, you can identify what the Parameter Model is and then proceed to use this model in the connection by filling in the required fields.

Note: All of the Models from the image below are Sync Model and Connection as models from this endpoint GetQuantitativeMetricData are all required and contains relation.

After identifying all the required inputs and outputs, you can proceed with configuring the Writer function. Please note that the Writer function accepts a parameterized model only when the Model endpoint GetQuantitativeMetricData is used. Otherwise, the Writer function will detect the mismatch, and you will not be able to save the configuration. See image below:

But when you use the Parameter model as an input, since the Parameter Model is already defined from the creation of Connection, Writer function will not require you to perform a Field mapping.

If in any case you have accidentally mapped a field, then you will receive an information like the image below.

Once configured, you may now proceed on performing the Run of your Writer function with the calculated data records you have Written / Upload to Parameterized Model.

That concludes this blog post. I hope this helped demonstrate how SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model intelligently recognizes OData service structures, making configuration easier and more user‑friendly.

Thank you so much! Bye for now!

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: What's New as of 2026-04-06?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community, 

Welcome to this month’s What’s New blog post for SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Standard Model. With each monthly update, we continue to evolve the solution with a strong focus on stability, usability, and overall experience. 

Let’s take a look at what this month’s release brings. 

Note: This blog post contains images. In case the images are too small, kindly click on the image to view it with a better resolution. 

Administration > Settings > Optional Features  > New Implementation of Remote Function Adapter Python 

A new optional feature for Remote Function Adapter (RFA) Python function type is now available in the Administration Settings application. This feature introduces a new version of the underlying runtime engine used to run Python scripts in the RFA Python function type.   

The RFA Python function type lets you run custom Python scripts to enhance your model with advanced calculations beyond standard functions. It processes input data, runs the script, and returns results to the model. For more information, refer to the official documentation on the SAP Help Portal.  

Administrators can use this opportunity and activate the new RFA Python implementation to evaluate and test it before it becomes part of the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM) application by default. We recommend you to activate the new implementation in development and testing environments first, to ensure your existing RFA Python functions are still working as expected before rolling it out to productive environments.   

RFA Python Changes  

This update introduces version 2 of the underlying Python runtime engine with upgraded platform and Python versions.   

Runtime Engine Comparison  

 Component  

RFA Python V1  

RFA Python V2  

Python  

3.9.23  

3.13.2 (new major version)  

Platform  

Linux x86-64  

WASI  

Built-In Libraries  

  • cmath  
  • math  
  • operator  
  • re  
  • time  
  • datetime  
  • decimal  
  • fractions  
  • ssl  
  • json  
  • urllib.parse  
  • cmath  
  • math  
  • operator  
  • re  
  • time  
  • datetime  
  • decimal  
  • fractions  
  • ssl  
  • json  
  • urllib.parse  

  Updated and Newly Supported External Python Packages  

The following table lists the officially supported Python library versions in the new implementation compared to the ones that were supported in the previous implementation. In addition to updated libraries, the new implementation supports two new libraries: httpx and urllib3.   

  For detailed release notes on each library, refer to the linked official documentation.    

 Library  

RFA Python V1  

RFA Python V2  

Release Notes  

httpx  

n/a  

0.28.1 (new library)  

   

numpy  

1.26.4  

2.2.5 (new major version)  

NumPy Release Notes  

pandas  

1.5.3  

2.3.2 (new major version)  

Pandas Release Notes  

requests  

2.32.4  

2.32.4  

   

scikit-learn  

1.5.2  

1.7.0  

Scikit Learn Release Highlights  

scipy  

1.13.1  

1.14.1  

SciPy Release Notes  

simplejson  

3.20.1  

3.20.1  

   

urllib3  

n/a  

2.5.0 (new library)  

  

  How to Activate the Optional Feature  

Administrators can activate the new implementation by completing the following steps:  

  1. Navigate to Administration > Settings section
  2. Scroll to the Optional Features section  
  3. Switch the toggle for Use new Remote Function Adapter Python implementation  
  4. Choose Save in the header toolbar to save your changes 

After saving, the new RFA Python implementation is activated.   

User Notifications  

Both modelers and processing users will see banners informing them about this optional feature:  

  • Before activation:   
    A banner indicates that a new implementation is available.  

  • After activation:   
    A banner confirms that the new implementation is active.  

Recommendation for Modelers  

If you use the RFA Python function type in your models, we recommend contacting your administrator to activate this feature in your development or testing environments. This allows you to validate your existing Python scripts against the updated Python version and library changes listed above and adjust them if needed.  

 Built-In Help  

The Built-In Help (SAP Companion) is now available in additional screens of the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model. Users can now access contextual in-app guidance directly from the following screens:  

  • Application Monitor  

  • Process Monitor  

  • Process Scheduler  

  • Teams Management  

  • Users Management  

  • Administration Settings  

  • Data Management Screen  

This enhancement helps users quickly find relevant guidance directly within the screens where they work.  

Process Scheduler > Notifications  

The new enhancement in Notifications ensures that users in the performer and reviewer groups receive an in-app or email notification whenever an activity is scheduled in the Process Scheduler.  

Joule | AI Enhancements 

A new Fullscreen view is now available for the Joule chat panel, providing a more spacious layout for conversations. This enhancement improves readability while maintaining the same Joule functionality as the standard right-hand side panel.  

 Modeling Screen  

One of the most impactful enhancements that is newly introduced in the Modeling application is the Mapping of Source Fields into Environment Fields capability for Model Table HANA and all Model View functions. This feature enables a controlled and consistent mapping between source fields and environment fields , which are used across functions and rules.  

To read more about this enhancement, visit the blogpost: SAP PaPM Cloud – Standard Model: Mapping of Source Fields into Environment Fields  

That’s all for this update. 

We hope this release helps further enhance your experience with SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model. If you’d like to explore previous updates or related content, feel free to browse the available tags associated with this post. 

Stay tuned — more updates are coming soon!

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Universal Model What’s New as of 2026-04-06
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

As we transition into the second quarter of the year, it’s the perfect moment to showcase the updates and enhancements introduced during February and March 2026. This edition of What’s New provides an overview of the latest features and improvements in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM).

If you’d like to revisit previous updates, feel free to explore posts under the PaPMCloudUMWhatsNew tag.

With that, let’s dive into what’s new for February and March.

 

Enhanced Authorization Control for Runtime Artifacts

SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Universal Model (SAP PaPM Cloud UM) now offers enhanced authorization control for runtime artifacts, ensuring that users can access only the information relevant to their assigned roles.

With this enhancement, access permissions are applied consistently to runtime artifacts. This means that what users can see and work with is aligned with their existing authorizations, creating a clear and consistent experience across the application.

This behavior is applied across the following application groups: Model, Process, Reports, and System. Lists, details, and available actions reflect only the objects that users are permitted to access.

Key Highlights

  • Runtime artifacts (e.g., Environments) are displayed based on user authorizations
  • Value help lists display only authorized artifacts, even when no filters are applied
  • Copy and Copy To actions are allowed only when the user has access to the selected target

For example, the user is assigned to a role collection called “Model Viewer” in SAP BTP, which contains the role MODEL_READ and the user attribute READQAMUSER.

 Image 1. Role Collection and roles assigned to the user in SAP BTP

 

Based on this assignment, the user can view only the runtime environments that match their authorization in the SAP PaPM Cloud UM application. Environments that are not relevant to the user’s role or team assignment are not displayed.

Image 2. Only authorized runtime environments are displayed in the list view.

Image 3. Value Help list displays the runtime environments the user is authorized to access.

This enhancement helps ensure controlled access while simplifying the user experience by displaying only relevant information.

For more information about roles, see: Role Templates in Universal Model.

 

Report

Environment Scope Toggle for Joule

This feature activates an intelligent QnA agent (Joule Bot) directly within the SAP PaPM Cloud UM Report Screen.

It empowers users to ask natural language questions about business environments, such as BEPS or Cost Allocation, and receive precise, context-aware answers.

How It Works:

By default, the Environment Scope toggle is switched off. Activate this option if you want to receive answers related to the business environment. 
 
The response behavior depends on the environment’s Runtime Status and the state of the Environment Scope toggle: 

  • Environment Scope: ON
    • Current: Joule provides answers based on the current environment’s runtime functions data. 
    • Inactive: No results are returned. 
  • Environment Scope: OFF
    • Joule provides answers based on the current report pages, regardless of the runtime status.

This setup allows users to easily switch between environment-specific insights and report-focused queries, ensuring relevant answers without unnecessary complexity.

 

Customizable Aggregated Charts with Grouping in Report Copilot

Users can now dynamically create customizable charts at runtime by specifying aggregations and row groupings on the selected grid columns. 

The generated Show Elements are automatically rendered with appropriate visualizations—such as line, bar, area, or pie charts—without requiring manual configuration.

How It Works:

  • As a prerequisite, ensure that a report is created and open it in Edit Mode.
  • The following prompts can be used as examples:
    • “Add a bar chart for [PlanGLData] Plan General Ledger Data with all row groups and max aggregated values”
    • “Add a Pie chart for [PlanGLData] Plan General Ledger Data with all row groups and min aggregated values”
    • “Add an Area chart for [PlanGLData] Plan General Ledger Data with all row groups and sum aggregated values”
    • “Add a Line chart for [PlanGLData] Plan General Ledger Data with all row groups and average aggregated values”

       

System

Enhanced Event Log when writing using SOAP Service 

In the SAP PaPM Cloud UM,  when users run the Writer function to send data to SAP S/4 HANA Cloud using the SOAP Service, an information prompt appears showing the message ID to indicate success. This is used as a reference when validating the writing from Universal Model onto the target platform SAP S/4 HANA Cloud. With this enhancement, the message ID is now displayed once per run of the Writer function using the SOAP Service.

Before

Message ID is shown twice when Writer function using SOAP Service is run.

After

Message ID is shown once when Writer function using SOAP Service is run.

 

 

Data Screen

Color Conditions in Adapt UI

We are introducing Color Conditions in Adapt UI, which is a new feature that enables users to apply dynamic background and/or text colors at the cell level based on configurable conditions.

Color Conditions are configured in Object Page → Adapt UI mode and are automatically reflected on both the Object Page and the Data Screen, ensuring a consistent visual experience across screens. Users can define multiple conditional rules for a field and configure background color, text color, or both. Conditions can also reference the values of other fields within the same row, enabling contextual visual highlighting.

When multiple conditions apply, the first matching rule takes priority. If the first rule defines only one color type (e.g., background only), the system continues evaluating subsequent rules to determine the missing color type and applies the combined result.

The configuration persists after reload and re-login, does not affect exported data, and can be easily removed to restore the default cell styling.  

 

Joule Design Enhancement

The Joule user interface has been enhanced with improved usability and flexibility.

The updated design includes an animated main image to provide a more dynamic interface, with the option to enable or disable animations directly in the Settings menu.

Users can now unlock the Joule window and reposition it anywhere on the screen, allowing for a more flexible workspace layout. In addition, the Chat menu now opens on click, improving interaction clarity and navigation.

An AI Notice has also been added to clearly inform users about AI-powered functionality within Joule.

 

Field Type Icons in Columns Panel 

We’re introducing an enhancement to the Columns Panel that improves how users identify field types.

Icons are now displayed beside each field, allowing users to instantly recognize the type of data they’re working with. This update improves clarity and reduces reliance on field names alone.

 

The following field types are now represented with corresponding icons:

                                         Field Types                                                   Icon

Amount

Association
Association to Many

 

 

 

Composition

 

 

 

Characteristics

 

 

 

In-Data Comment

 

 

Key Figure

 

 

Quantity

 

 

Icons are implemented consistently and behave the same across:

  • Function (Show Data)
  • Activity (Show Data)
  • Report pages (Show Element and Application Element)

This ensures a consistent experience for users whenever they interact with field selections.

Improved visual consistency and clear cues enable users to navigate and interpret data more efficiently, resulting in a smoother overall experience.

 

Preserved Group Expansion State After Sorting

This update improves the behavior of the Data Screen when working with grouped data and sorting. With this enhancement, the system now preserves the expanded and collapsed state of row groups when sorting is applied. Sorting actions now only reorder the data and no longer affect the visibility state of grouped rows.

The improvement applies to all grouping configurations within the Data Screen grid, including groups created via the Column Menu and those defined through the Column Pane (row grouping area). It supports all standard sorting actions such as “Sort Ascending”, “Sort Descending”, and “Clear Sort”, as well as master data sorting options like “Sort by Value”, “Sort by Text”, “Text First”, and “Text Last”. The behavior is consistent for both “Single Column” and “Multiple Column” group display modes.

As a result, users can now sort data without losing their current analytical context. Expanded groups remain expanded, and collapsed groups remain collapsed across all sorting operations, improving usability and efficiency when working with grouped datasets.

Please note that these improvements are also applied in Reports Elements, ensuring consistent behavior across the application.

 

Enhanced Long Text Handling 

This update introduces improved handling of long texts in Data Screen, ensuring a consistent and more readable user experience.

Long text is now fully visible by default, as rows automatically expand to fit the content and prevent truncation of wrapped text.

In Edit Mode, Large String fields are scrollable, allowing users to easily view and edit content without affecting the layout.

Paragraph structure (including line breaks) is preserved for Large String fields and displayed in the original format. Meanwhile, String fields continue to display text without paragraph formatting.

 The Show More / Show Less functionality on the Object Page allows users to easily expand or collapse long text fields.

 

Inline editing has been enhanced to support efficient maintenance of long text directly within the grid. For Large String fields, a pencil icon provides access to a dialog with a text area for more convenient editing. Formatting, including line breaks, is preserved after saving.

 

Additional layout flexibility is introduced through “Compact Rows” and extended column sizing options. Users can choose “Compact Rows” in the Column Menu to switch between fully expanded text (default) and a condensed single-line view, and save this preference as part of a layout.

Please note that these improvements are also applied in Reports Elements, ensuring consistent behavior across the application.

 

That’s a wrap for now!

We hope these new features and enhancements make your experience with Cloud UM even smoother. Keep an eye out for our monthly “What’s New” releases.

Thank you for sticking around! If you found this helpful, feel free to spread the word to others.

entry.title: Turn tax into strategy: Margin steering with SAP PaPM and SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Transfer pricing rarely gets the spotlight.  But it should.

In this partner webinar, we open the doors to a real SAP journey.  A shift from fixed license rates to a margin-based model powered by Transactional Net Margin Method (TNMM).  The goal was clear.  Align tax, profitability, and compliance with the realities of a cloud business.

You will see how SAP built an end-to-end solution using SAP Profitability and Performance Management, SAP Analytics Cloud, and Settlement Management.  Not theory.  Actual execution.

The result.  Better audit readiness.  Less manual work.  A model that scales as the business grows.

The thing is, margin steering is not just a finance exercise.  It gives your customers a way to stay within arm’s length ranges while keeping profitability predictable.

If you work with EPM, xP&A, or tax-driven transformations, this session will give you practical ideas you can apply fast.

Register now and see how strategy meets execution.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/apr/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013760.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPAnalyticsCloud #EPM #xP&A #TransferPricing

entry.title: SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model: Global Notification Settings
entry.content:
Hello Everyone!

I’m excited to share one of the latest enhancements in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM), which you can already experience in your tenants today. In this blog post, I’ll walk you through Global Notification Settings and show how they help you better manage and govern system notifications.

With growing collaboration across environments, processes, and teams in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM), notifications play an important role in keeping users informed about relevant system activities. However, when notification behavior is managed only at user level, system administrators have limited control over which updates should be mandatory, optional, or disabled across the system. 

Global Notification Settings address this by introducing centralized notification governance within the Settings screen, allowing administrators to define notification behavior at system level. This enhancement allows administrators to configure notification categories  as always sent, deactivated, or managed individually by end users, creating a balance between centralized control and user flexibility. 

This blog post provides an overview of Global Notification Settings, including the available configuration options, supported notification categories, and the notification governance model introduced in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Standard Model.

Why Do Global Notification Settings Matter? 

Global Notification Settings provide a centralized and scalable approach to notification management in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model. By defining notification behavior at system level, administrators can ensure consistent communication rules across the system while still allowing user flexibility where appropriate. 

This helps reduce missed critical updates, limit unnecessary notifications, and maintain predictable notification behavior across teams and delivery channels. As a result, both administrators and end users benefit from a clearer and more consistent notification experience..

Access and Required Roles 

Global Notification Settings are available through the Settings application and are intended for system-level administration. Access to this functionality is available to users assigned to roles that provide administrative privileges, such as: 

  • ADMIN_USER_ALL  
  • ADMIN_USER_RESTRICTED  
  • SETTINGS

Navigation path: Administration → Settings → Global Notification Settings

Note: For additional details regarding administration roles and their permissions, refer to the dedicated Administration Roles documentation: SAP Help Portal - Role Templates in Standard Model.

Global Notification Settings Configuration

This section is organized in such way to give administrators a clear overview of notification governance settings and allows configuration per notification category, including key elements:

  • Notification Channel Tabs
    Configuration is separated into two delivery channels:
    • In-App Notifications tab – used to define how notifications are delivered within the application interface.
    • Email Notifications tab – controls how notifications are delivered via email communication.

Each channel can be configured independently, allowing administrators to define separate rules for in-app and email notifications, or email notifications can follow the same configuration as in-app notifications by marking the Reuse settings from In-App checkbox on the Email Notifications tab:

When this checkbox is marked, email notification settings inherit the in-app configuration, and the individual notification type configuration options in the Email Notifications tab become grayed out and non-editable.

  • Information Banner
    An informational message is displayed below the tabs to indicate that changes applied within Global Notification Settings affect all users across the system.

  • Notification Types
    The following notification types are categories of events that can initiate system notifications. Each category includes specific actions or changes that may generate in-app or email notifications based on the configured settings:

    • Activity Changes – notifications initiated  by workflow-related actions such as Submit, Reject, Approve, Complete, or when a new comment is added, updated, or deleted  .

    • Private Environment Changes – notifications initiated when a private environment is created, updated, deleted, imported, overwritten, assigned to teams, or changed from “Private” to “Public.”

    • Process Changes – notifications initiated by process state updates, including transitions such as Open, Deployed, Suspended, Completed, or Canceled.

    • Scheduled Jobs – notifications initiated by scheduled job lifecycle events, including creation, start, completion, or when a scheduled job becomes due.

    • Team Changes – notifications initiated when a user is assigned or unassigned from a team or child team, or when a team is marked or unmarked as obsolete.

  •  Notification Delivery Rules
    Each notification type includes a dropdown option that allows administrators to define how notifications are managed and delivered for that category:

    • Allow users to manage settings – notification delivery for the selected notification type is controlled by the end user through User Preferences. Users can notifications based on their personal preference.

      Note: For additional details regarding User Preferences, refer to the dedicated documentation: SAP Help Portal - User Preferences in Standard Model. 

    • Always Send – notifications are automatically delivered to users whenever an event associated with the selected notification type is initiated. These notifications are enforced by Global Notification Settings and cannot be deactivated in User Preferences.

    • Never Send – notifications are blocked for the selected notification type, meaning users will not receive them when related events are initiated and these notifications remain deactivated regardless of user’s configuration.

Notification Delivery Rule Scenarios

Regardless of whether the notification is delivered through in-pp or email, Global Notification Settings behavior is determined by the administrator’s configuration for each notification type.

Always Send
When a notification type is configured as Always Send, notifications are enforced at system level regardless of user preferences. Users automatically receive notifications for initiated events, and the related options in User Preferences appear checked, greyed out, and non-editable.

Allow Users to Manage Settings
When a notification type is configured as Allow Users to Manage Settings, notification delivery depends on the user’s configuration. Users can independently activate or deactivate notifications, and the related options in User Preferences remain editable and not greyed out.

Never Send
When a notification type is configured as Never Send, notifications are disabled regardless of user’s configuration. Users cannot activate notifications for that category, and the related options in User Preferences appear unchecked, greyed out, and non-editable.

I hope this article has given you a clear understanding of Global Notification Settings in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud – Standard Model. These enhancements are designed to give administrators better control while keeping users consistently informed. 

 
Please stay tuned for the next blog post!

entry.title: Integration Paths Between SAP PaPM Cloud and SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

This short blog post explores the integration paths between SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud (SAP PaPM Cloud) and SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC).

SAP BDC is architected to directly integrate with a defined set of supported consumer applications. This design is outlined in the official documentation on Data Products in SAP Business Data Cloud. In this context, SAP PaPM Cloud complements the overall architecture by leveraging its flexibility to integrate through these SAP BDC–supported consumers.
